text: A good day to you, my name is Shaoxiong. This is part one on a series of how to perform laparoscopic conventional and D-tree right hemicolectomy.
I will not be talking about the literature behind the rationale of performing a D-tree right hemicolectomy as the purpose of the video is to detail the technical aspects of the surgery itself.
In part one, I'll be going through the various approaches to performing a right hemicolectomy and how to perform right hemicolon mobilization.
This is sufficient to perform a conventional right hemicolectomy.
In part two, I'll be going into detail of the relevant lymphovascular anatomy, how to perform the central nodal dissection, as well as final specimen evaluation.
Finally, in part three, I will comment on a summary operation.
In Singapore, D-tree right hemicolectomy is not yet widely practised. However, I believe that it is only a matter of time before it becomes standard of care.

I would like to emphasise that although the videos concentrate on the technical details of surgery, case selection and perioperative management is just as, or even more important than doing the surgery itself.
The surgery of cancer is a surgery of lymphatics. Here I will define what vessels are required to be taken for a right hemicolectomy.
I refer to this article written by Professor Choi and his team in Kyungpook National University Hospital, South Korea.
For cancers of the cecum, I would take the iliocollic artery, the right collic artery if present, and the right branch of middle collic artery.
For an ascending colon tumour, the iliocolic artery and the right colic artery, if present, should be taken. In addition, the middle colic artery should also be taken.
For hepatic flexure and transverse colon tumours, the iliocolic artery is not so important to be taken. However, the right colic artery, if present, and the middle colic artery should be taken.
I will now describe the various approaches to right hemicolectomy.
Probably the most popular approach to a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy is a vessel-first, medial-to-lateral approach.
Less commonly performed is the inferior lateral-to-medial approach.
And a supracolic lateral-to-medial approach is probably the least commonly performed.
I will first talk about the vessel-first medial-to-lateral approach.
The surgeon lifts the ileocolic pedicle and incises below it to develop a plane underneath, revealing the underlying tissue layers.
The surgeon divides the artery and vein, continuing the dissection plane laterally to mobilize the right hemicolon.
Here is a video on the vessel first medial to lateral approach.
The surgeon holds up the ileocolic pedicle, revealing the duodenum visible through the mesenteric window, located just superior to the ileocolic pedicle.
The assistant holds up the ileocolic pedicle near the cecum, tenting it superiorly. The surgeon makes an incision inferiorly using sharp dissection beneath the pedicle.
The surgeon cuts through the mesentery, progressing until reaching the plane between the retroperitoneum and the colonic mesentery, continuing the dissection to mobilize the right hemicolon.
The surgeon carefully skeletonizes the ileocolic pedicle, identifying the artery and vein. The artery is ligated and divided, followed by the same procedure for the vein.
The surgeon lifts the mesentery by holding the cut end of the ileocolic pedicle and performs medial to lateral mobilization using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques.

The surgeon continues dissection until reaching the bowel laterally and superiorly, while medially detaching the duodenum from the mesentery. The procedure concludes with the removal of the lateral attachments.
The surgeon begins the inferior approach to mobilize the right hemicolon, focusing on the dissection and separation of the colonic mesentery from the retroperitoneal structures.
The patient is positioned in a head-down orientation while the assistant elevates the cecum, facilitating exposure for further dissection.
The surgeon makes an incision inferiorly and carefully seeks a plane between the mesentery and the retroperitoneum, ensuring the gonadal vessels and ureter are not injured during the dissection.
The surgeon follows the dissection plane cranially, facilitating the mobilization of the right hemicolon, with visible separation of tissue layers.

The patient is positioned head down in the Trendelenburg position, with the small bowel flipped to the left upper quadrant. The surgeon lifts the cecum and makes an inferior incision to separate the bowel from the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon develops the plane between the mesentery and the retroperitoneum, facilitating the mobilization of the mesentery away from the head of the pancreas. This approach may also ease the dissection of DIII central nodal tissue.
The surgeon transitions to the supracolic approach for mobilizing the right hemicolon, with the surgical field showing the anatomical structures above the transverse colon.

The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg, and the surgeon enters the lesser sac by incising the lesser omentum. The gastroepiploic vessels are traced down to the base of the transverse colon mesentery.

The surgeon develops a dissection plane along the superior border of the transverse colon, facilitating separation from the duodenum. This is followed by the takedown of the hepatic flexure and lateral to medial mobilisation of the upper right hemicolon.

Upon reaching the base of the transverse colon mesentery, the gastrocolic trunk vessels are visible. The surgeon identifies and prepares to divide the colic branch of the gastrocolic trunk of Henle, known as the registras vein.

The surgeon divides the colic branch of the trunk of Henle, allowing the transverse colon to be flipped down. This maneuver exposes the C-loop of the duodenum and the head of the pancreas, revealing these anatomical structures for further dissection.

The patient is placed in the Trendelenburg position, and the surgeon completes the mobilization of the right hemicolon through an inferior approach, adjusting the orientation for optimal access to the surgical site.
The video shows the supracolic approach to right hemicolon mobilization. The patient is positioned in a reverse Trendelenburg position with the right side elevated.
The assistant elevates the stomach or gastroepiploic vessels along the greater curvature, while the surgeon incises the omentum below these vessels to enter the lesser sac.
The surgeon continues dissection along the gastroepiploic vessels as they extend towards the pancreas, occasionally encountering small branches to the colon that require division.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection to identify a bloodless plane between the duodenum and the transverse colon mesentery, progressing towards the hepatic flexure. Near the base of the transverse colon mesentery, a venous tributary is observed joining the gastroepiploic vein from the gastrocolic trunk of Henle.
The surgeon applies downward traction on the transverse colon, which risks avulsion from the gastrocolic trunk, potentially causing significant bleeding.
The surgeon carefully isolates and divides the tissue, exposing the head of the pancreas. The head of the pancreas is now clearly visible following meticulous dissection.

The divided colic branch of the gastrocolic trunk and the gastroepiploic vein are visible. Further dissection progresses towards separating the tissue at Toldt's fascia, facilitating the takedown of the hepatic flexure.
The surgical team holds the ascending colon and retracts it inferiorly, facilitating the takedown of the hepatic flexure. The hepatic flexure is visibly being separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the dissection, exposing the third part of the duodenum. The patient is then placed in the Trendelenburg position, allowing for the inferior mobilization of the right hemicolon.
I have now gone through the three methods of right hemicolon mobilization and will conclude part one of how to perform a right hemicolectomy.
Please look for part two, which focuses on how to perform central vessel ligation in a D-tree right hemicolectomy.
